# Piano Tutor ‚Äî Learning Guide

A path from your first notes to early intermediate playing (~ABRSM Grade 4), using
everything in the app and pointing to the outside learning worth adding along the way.
Nothing is tracked and nothing unlocks: read a stage, practice from it, and move on when
it feels secure. Twenty focused minutes a day outruns a two-hour Sunday.

## Stage 1 ‚Äî Foundations

*Before Grade 1*

Get comfortable at the keyboard: find notes by name, play five-finger patterns with curved fingers and a relaxed hand, keep a steady pulse, and read your first notes on the staff. Everything else in this guide builds on these habits, so take your time here.

### You're working towards

- Play five-finger positions in C, G and F with either hand, evenly and without looking at your hands for every note
- Name any white key instantly; find sharps and flats from it
- Clap and tap steady quarter and half notes with the metronome
- Recognize the treble and bass staff and read notes in the C five-finger range
- Hear the difference between two notes moving up and moving down

### Practice material

The `#/...` paths are in-app links ‚Äî open the app and paste one after its URL, or find the same item from the screen it names.

**Technique**

- Five-finger position in C ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=five-finger-C`
- Five-finger position in G ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=five-finger-G`
- Five-finger position in F ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=five-finger-F`
- Hanon No. 1 ‚Äî first finger exercise ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=hanon-1`

**Pieces**

- Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star ‚Äî `#/songs?song=twinkle-twinkle`
- Mary Had a Little Lamb ‚Äî `#/songs?song=mary-had-a-little-lamb`
- Ode to Joy (Beethoven) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=ode-to-joy`

**Ear**

- Intervals ‚Äî level 1 (up, down, or the same?) ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=intervals&level=1`

**Rhythm**

- Rhythm ‚Äî level 1 (quarters, halves, whole notes) ‚Äî `#/rhythm?level=1`

**Sight-reading**

- Sight-reading ‚Äî level 1 (C position, one note at a time) ‚Äî `#/practice?sight=1`

### The staff, the clefs, and note names

Music is written on a five-line staff. The treble clef (ùÑû) marks the staff your right hand usually reads; the bass clef (ùÑ¢) the left. Middle C sits between them on its own small ledger line. Notes climb line-space-line-space; every eighth note the letter names repeat. Learn a few anchor notes (middle C, treble G, bass F) and count neighbours from them rather than memorizing every line at once.

- Quiz: name the note on the staff ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=note-naming&level=1`
- Note Detector ‚Äî play a key, see its name and notation ‚Äî `#/tuner`
- Practice reading single notes in C position ‚Äî `#/practice?sight=1`

### Counting time

Rhythm is arithmetic you can feel: a whole note lasts four beats, a half note two, a quarter one. The time signature tells you how many beats fill a bar. Count out loud ‚Äî "1, 2, 3, 4" ‚Äî while you play; the notes that feel long are the ones you are counting through. The metronome is not a critic, it is a dance partner: put it on 60 and land your quarters exactly with the clicks.

- Tap level-1 rhythms against the metronome ‚Äî `#/rhythm?level=1`
- Quiz: hear a rhythm, pick its notation ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=rhythm-dictation&level=1`

### A week at this stage (~20 min/day)

- 5 min ‚Äî five-finger positions (rotate C, G, F; hands separately)
- 5 min ‚Äî Hanon No. 1, slowly, watching for tension
- 5 min ‚Äî one song, small sections at a time
- 3 min ‚Äî rhythm level 1 with the metronome
- 2 min ‚Äî interval quiz level 1

### Beyond the app

- [Faber ‚Äî Adult Piano Adventures Book 1](https://pianoadventures.com/products/adult-piano-adventures-all-in-one-course-book-1/) ‚Äî The best-structured adult method book. Work through it alongside this app ‚Äî it teaches reading and musicality the app can only drill.
- [Alfred's Basic Adult All-in-One Course](https://www.alfred.com/alfreds-basic-adult-all-in-one-course-book-1/p/00-5753/) ‚Äî The main alternative to Faber; pick one method book and stick with it.
- [Bill Hilton (YouTube)](https://www.youtube.com/@BillHilton) ‚Äî Calm, practical piano tutorials ‚Äî excellent on technique, practice habits and beginner theory.
- [musictheory.net ‚Äî The Staff, Clefs, and Ledger Lines](https://www.musictheory.net/lessons) ‚Äî Free interactive theory lessons; the first few cover exactly this stage.

### Move on when‚Ä¶

- You can play all three five-finger positions evenly at 70 bpm without watching your hands
- You can play Ode to Joy through without stopping
- You can read any note in the C five-finger range within a couple of seconds

---

## Stage 2 ‚Äî First scales and full pieces

*‚âà ABRSM Grade 1*

Thumb-under scales, your first arpeggios, and playing hands together. The pieces get longer and use both hands at once ‚Äî the big skill of this stage is keeping going: steady tempo, eyes ahead, no stopping to fix mistakes.

### You're working towards

- Play C, G, D and F major scales one octave, hands separately then together
- Play root-position arpeggios in C, G and F
- Play I‚ÄìIV‚ÄìV‚ÄìI cadences in C ‚Äî your first chords
- Play a complete piece hands together at a steady tempo
- Name major 2nds, 3rds, 5ths and octaves by ear

### Practice material

The `#/...` paths are in-app links ‚Äî open the app and paste one after its URL, or find the same item from the screen it names.

**Technique**

- C major scale (1 & 2 octaves, hands together) ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-C-major`
- G major scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-G-major`
- D major scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-D-major`
- F major scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-F-major`
- C major arpeggio ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=arpeggio-C-major`
- G major arpeggio ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=arpeggio-G-major`
- F major arpeggio ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=arpeggio-F-major`
- I‚ÄìIV‚ÄìV‚ÄìI cadence in C ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=cadence-C`

**Pieces**

- Jingle Bells ‚Äî `#/songs?song=jingle-bells`
- When the Saints Go Marching In ‚Äî `#/songs?song=when-the-saints`
- Minuet in G (Petzold) ‚Äî stretch piece ‚Äî `#/songs?song=minuet-in-g`

**Ear**

- Intervals ‚Äî level 2 ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=intervals&level=2`
- Chord qualities ‚Äî level 1 (major vs minor) ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=chords&level=1`
- Play it back ‚Äî level 1 ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=echo&level=1`

**Rhythm**

- Rhythm ‚Äî level 2 (eighth notes, rests, off-beats) ‚Äî `#/rhythm?level=2`

**Sight-reading**

- Sight-reading ‚Äî level 2 (G, D and F positions, skips) ‚Äî `#/practice?sight=2`

### Key signatures: your first sharps and flats

A key signature at the start of each line tells you which notes are always sharp or flat. G major has one sharp (F‚ôØ); F major one flat (B‚ô≠); D major two sharps (F‚ôØ, C‚ôØ). Rather than reading each accidental, think "I am in G, so every F is sharp". Scales make key signatures physical: your fingers learn where the black keys live in each key.

- Quiz: read the key signature ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=key-signature&level=1`
- G major in the scale library ‚Äî `#/scales?root=G&type=major`
- F major in the scale library ‚Äî `#/scales?root=F&type=major`

### Intervals: the distance between notes

An interval is the distance between two notes, counted in letter names: C to E is a 3rd, C to G a 5th. Reading by interval ("up a 3rd") is faster than naming every note, and hearing intervals is how melodies become predictable. The interval quiz trains your ear; sight-reading trains your eye to see the same distances on the staff.

- Interval ear quiz ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=intervals&level=2`
- Quiz: intervals on the staff ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=interval-staff&level=1`
- Read by interval in sight-reading ‚Äî `#/practice?sight=2`

### A week at this stage (~30 min/day)

- 8 min ‚Äî scales C/G/D/F (one key per day, hands separate then together)
- 4 min ‚Äî arpeggios and the C cadence
- 10 min ‚Äî your current piece, in sections, slow then at tempo
- 4 min ‚Äî sight-reading level 2 (one new melody a day)
- 4 min ‚Äî ear training (alternate intervals and chords)

### Beyond the app

- [Faber ‚Äî Adult Piano Adventures Book 1](https://pianoadventures.com/products/adult-piano-adventures-all-in-one-course-book-1/) ‚Äî The best-structured adult method book. Work through it alongside this app ‚Äî it teaches reading and musicality the app can only drill.
- [Bill Hilton (YouTube)](https://www.youtube.com/@BillHilton) ‚Äî Calm, practical piano tutorials ‚Äî excellent on technique, practice habits and beginner theory.
- [ABRSM Piano syllabus](https://www.abrsm.org/en-gb/our-exams/piano-exams) ‚Äî The graded exam repertoire lists this guide is aligned to ‚Äî browse pieces at your stage even if you never sit an exam.
- [ABRSM Grade 1 piano pieces (current list)](https://www.abrsm.org/en-gb/our-exams/piano-exams/piano-grade-1) ‚Äî Browse real Grade 1 pieces to supplement the app ‚Äî most are on IMSLP or in the exam book.
- [r/pianolearning](https://www.reddit.com/r/pianolearning/) ‚Äî Friendly community for feedback, practice questions and motivation.

### Move on when‚Ä¶

- C, G, D and F major scales are secure hands together at 72 bpm
- You can play Jingle Bells or When the Saints start to finish, hands together, without stopping
- The I‚ÄìIV‚ÄìV‚ÄìI cadence in C feels like one gesture per chord, not three separate notes

---

## Stage 3 ‚Äî Minor keys and independence

*‚âà ABRSM Grade 2*

Minor scales bring new colours; broken chords and two-octave scales stretch your technique; and the repertoire starts demanding real independence between the hands ‚Äî a melody that sings over an accompaniment that stays out of its way.

### You're working towards

- Play A, E and D harmonic minor scales, hands separately then together
- Play broken chords through all inversions in the common keys
- Play cadences in G and F as well as C
- Play pieces where the hands do genuinely different things
- Recognize major, minor, diminished and augmented chords by ear

### Practice material

The `#/...` paths are in-app links ‚Äî open the app and paste one after its URL, or find the same item from the screen it names.

**Technique**

- A harmonic minor scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-A-harmonic-minor`
- E harmonic minor scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-E-harmonic-minor`
- D harmonic minor scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-D-harmonic-minor`
- C major broken chords ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=broken-C-major`
- A minor broken chords ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=broken-A-minor`
- G major broken chords ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=broken-G-major`
- I‚ÄìIV‚ÄìV‚ÄìI cadence in G ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=cadence-G`
- I‚ÄìIV‚ÄìV‚ÄìI cadence in F ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=cadence-F`

**Pieces**

- Melody, Op. 68 No. 1 (Schumann) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=schumann-melodie`
- La Candeur, Op. 100 No. 1 (Burgm√ºller) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=burgmuller-la-candeur`
- Old French Song (Tchaikovsky) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=old-french-song`
- St. James Infirmary ‚Äî `#/songs?song=st-james-infirmary`
- 12-Bar Blues in C ‚Äî `#/songs?song=twelve-bar-blues-c`
- F√ºr Elise ‚Äî theme (Beethoven) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=fur-elise`
- The Entertainer ‚Äî easy arrangement (Joplin) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=the-entertainer-easy`

**Ear**

- Chord qualities ‚Äî level 2 (adds diminished & augmented) ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=chords&level=2`
- Intervals ‚Äî level 3 ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=intervals&level=3`
- Play it back ‚Äî level 2 ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=echo&level=2`

**Rhythm**

- Rhythm ‚Äî level 3 (dotted rhythms & syncopation) ‚Äî `#/rhythm?level=3`
- Rhythm dictation ‚Äî hear it, pick the notation ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=rhythm-dictation&level=2`

**Sight-reading**

- Sight-reading ‚Äî level 3 (bass clef, accidentals) ‚Äî `#/practice?sight=3`

### Relative minors: two keys, one signature

Every key signature serves two keys: a major and its relative minor, three semitones below (C major / A minor share no sharps or flats). Minor pieces usually raise the 7th note as an accidental in the score ‚Äî that raised note is why the harmonic minor scale sounds the way it does, and why you practice it.

- A harmonic minor in the scale library ‚Äî `#/scales?root=A&type=harmonic%20minor`
- A natural minor for comparison ‚Äî `#/scales?root=A&type=natural%20minor`

### Primary chords: I, IV and V

Almost every simple piece is built from three chords: the tonic (I), subdominant (IV) and dominant (V), built on the 1st, 4th and 5th notes of the key. The cadence drills teach your hands their shapes; the blues teaches you to hear them go by ‚Äî a 12-bar blues is nothing but I, IV and V in a fixed order.

- Cadences in G ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=cadence-G`
- Quiz: which chord is V? ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=chord-function&level=1`
- Quiz: spell the chord ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=chord-spelling&level=1`
- Hear I‚ÄìIV‚ÄìV in the 12-bar blues ‚Äî `#/songs?song=twelve-bar-blues-c`

### A week at this stage (~40 min/day)

- 10 min ‚Äî scales: rotate the harmonic minors and keep two majors warm
- 5 min ‚Äî broken chords and cadences (one key per day)
- 15 min ‚Äî repertoire: one classical piece and one blues/jazz piece in parallel
- 5 min ‚Äî sight-reading level 3
- 5 min ‚Äî ear training (chords level 2 is the priority)

### Beyond the app

- [Faber ‚Äî Adult Piano Adventures Book 2](https://pianoadventures.com/products/adult-piano-adventures-all-in-one-course-book-2/) ‚Äî Continues where Book 1 ends ‚Äî roughly the Grade 1‚Äì2 span of this guide.
- [PianoTV (YouTube)](https://www.youtube.com/@PianoTV) ‚Äî Graded repertoire suggestions, theory and practice advice organized by RCM/ABRSM level.
- [ABRSM Piano syllabus](https://www.abrsm.org/en-gb/our-exams/piano-exams) ‚Äî The graded exam repertoire lists this guide is aligned to ‚Äî browse pieces at your stage even if you never sit an exam.
- [IMSLP (Petrucci Music Library)](https://imslp.org) ‚Äî Free public-domain sheet music ‚Äî every classical piece in this app and thousands more.
- [r/pianolearning](https://www.reddit.com/r/pianolearning/) ‚Äî Friendly community for feedback, practice questions and motivation.

### Move on when‚Ä¶

- Harmonic minor scales in A, E and D are secure hands together
- You can play F√ºr Elise (theme) with the left hand quiet under the melody
- You can hear whether a chord is major, minor or diminished most of the time

---

## Stage 4 ‚Äî Fluency in more keys

*‚âà ABRSM Grade 3*

The technique net widens: more minor keys, two-octave playing as the norm, and repertoire with flowing sixteenth-note figuration and walking bass lines. Rhythm work reaches swing. This is where daily technique practice starts to pay off audibly in the pieces.

### You're working towards

- Play major scales with black-key tonics (B‚ô≠, E‚ô≠, A major) and more minors
- Play the chromatic scale and your first contrary-motion scales
- Play melodic minor ‚Äî different notes up and down
- Play arpeggios from black-key roots (B‚ô≠, E‚ô≠ major)
- Keep a walking bass steady under right-hand chords
- Identify seventh chords by ear

### Practice material

The `#/...` paths are in-app links ‚Äî open the app and paste one after its URL, or find the same item from the screen it names.

**Technique**

- B‚ô≠ major scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-Bb-major`
- E‚ô≠ major scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-Eb-major`
- A major scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-A-major`
- B harmonic minor scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-B-harmonic-minor`
- A melodic minor scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-A-melodic-minor`
- D melodic minor scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-D-melodic-minor`
- Chromatic scale from C ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=chromatic-C`
- C major in contrary motion ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=contrary-C-major`
- G major in contrary motion ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=contrary-G-major`
- B‚ô≠ major arpeggio (black-key root) ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=arpeggio-Bb-major`
- A minor arpeggio ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=arpeggio-A-minor`
- D minor arpeggio ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=arpeggio-D-minor`

**Pieces**

- Arabesque, Op. 100 No. 2 (Burgm√ºller) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=burgmuller-arabesque`
- The Wild Horseman, Op. 68 No. 8 (Schumann) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=schumann-wild-rider`
- Sonatina Op. 36 No. 1, 1st movement (Clementi) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=clementi-sonatina-1`
- Prelude in C (Bach, WTC I) ‚Äî opening ‚Äî `#/songs?song=bach-prelude-c`
- Jazz Blues in G with walking bass ‚Äî `#/songs?song=jazz-blues-walking-g`

**Ear**

- Chord qualities ‚Äî level 3 (adds 7th chords) ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=chords&level=3`
- Intervals ‚Äî level 4 (all twelve) ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=intervals&level=4`
- Cadences ‚Äî perfect or plagal? ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=cadence&level=1`
- Play it back ‚Äî level 3 ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=echo&level=3`

**Rhythm**

- Rhythm ‚Äî level 4 (swing, Charleston, comping) ‚Äî `#/rhythm?level=4`

**Sight-reading**

- Sight-reading ‚Äî level 4 (eighths & dotted rhythms) ‚Äî `#/practice?sight=4`

### Seventh chords and the circle of fifths

Add one more third on top of a triad and you get a seventh chord ‚Äî the sound of jazz and of every dominant that pulls home to its tonic. The circle of fifths orders all twelve keys by how many sharps or flats they carry; moving one step around it adds one accidental. Practice scales in circle order (C, G, D, A‚Ä¶ / C, F, B‚ô≠, E‚ô≠‚Ä¶) and the pattern becomes second nature.

- G7 in the chord library ‚Äî `#/chords?root=G&quality=dominant%207th`
- Hear 7th chords in the ear quiz ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=chords&level=3`
- Quiz: spell the 7th chords ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=chord-spelling&level=3`
- Quiz: key signatures around the circle ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=key-signature&level=3`

### Three flavours of minor

Minor is not one scale but a family. Natural minor is what the key signature gives you; harmonic minor raises the 7th so the dominant chord can pull home; melodic minor also raises the 6th on the way up so the line climbs smoothly, then relaxes back to natural on the way down. Composers mix all three freely inside a single minor-key piece ‚Äî that is why minor pieces are speckled with accidentals.

- A melodic minor ‚Äî hear both directions ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-A-melodic-minor`
- Compare in the scale library ‚Äî `#/scales?root=A&type=harmonic%20minor`

### A week at this stage (~45 min/day)

- 12 min ‚Äî scales & arpeggios in circle-of-fifths order, two octaves hands together
- 18 min ‚Äî repertoire: Bach prelude in sections; jazz blues at a slow, dead-steady tempo
- 5 min ‚Äî rhythm level 4 (swing feels strange until suddenly it does not)
- 5 min ‚Äî sight-reading level 4
- 5 min ‚Äî ear training level 3+

### Beyond the app

- [Consider a human teacher](https://www.abrsm.org/en-gb/find-a-music-teacher) ‚Äî From this stage on, occasional lessons (even monthly) catch technique problems an app cannot hear ‚Äî posture, tension, tone. Worth it if you can.
- [PianoTV (YouTube)](https://www.youtube.com/@PianoTV) ‚Äî Graded repertoire suggestions, theory and practice advice organized by RCM/ABRSM level.
- [Josh Wright (YouTube)](https://www.youtube.com/@joshwrightpiano) ‚Äî Concert pianist; deep dives on technique problems once you have the basics.
- [ABRSM Piano syllabus](https://www.abrsm.org/en-gb/our-exams/piano-exams) ‚Äî The graded exam repertoire lists this guide is aligned to ‚Äî browse pieces at your stage even if you never sit an exam.
- [RCM Piano syllabus](https://www.rcmusic.com/en/learning/academic-programs/certificate-program/piano) ‚Äî The North American equivalent of ABRSM; its repertoire lists are a goldmine of level-appropriate pieces.
- [IMSLP (Petrucci Music Library)](https://imslp.org) ‚Äî Free public-domain sheet music ‚Äî every classical piece in this app and thousands more.

### Move on when‚Ä¶

- Two-octave hands-together scales feel like the normal way to play a scale
- The Bach prelude flows without accents inside the figuration
- You can hold the walking bass steady while the right hand comps off the beat

---

## Stage 5 ‚Äî Early intermediate

*‚âà ABRSM Grade 4*

All keys are now in play, jazz harmony enters properly with the ii‚ÄìV‚ÄìI, and the repertoire is real intermediate music: Canon in D, a Chopin prelude, stride patterns. From here the path is less about new mechanics and more about sound: voicing, phrasing, and control ‚Äî which is exactly where a human teacher matters most.

### You're working towards

- Play any major or harmonic minor scale, two octaves hands together
- Play ii‚ÄìV‚ÄìI progressions with guide tones in several keys
- Play the blues scale and improvise simple phrases over a 12-bar form
- Learn a piece of several pages and keep it in your fingers over weeks
- Recognize all common chord qualities and wide intervals by ear

### Practice material

The `#/...` paths are in-app links ‚Äî open the app and paste one after its URL, or find the same item from the screen it names.

**Technique**

- B major scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-B-major`
- F‚ôØ major scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-F%23-major`
- D‚ô≠ major scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-Db-major`
- C‚ôØ harmonic minor scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-C%23-harmonic-minor`
- E melodic minor scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=scale-E-melodic-minor`
- Chromatic scale from A ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=chromatic-A`
- E major in contrary motion ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=contrary-E-major`
- D‚ô≠ major arpeggio ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=arpeggio-Db-major`
- F‚ôØ minor arpeggio ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=arpeggio-F%23-minor`
- ii‚ÄìV‚ÄìI in C with guide tones ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=jazz-251-C`
- ii‚ÄìV‚ÄìI in F ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=jazz-251-F`
- C blues scale ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=jazz-blues-scale-C`
- 12-bar blues comping in C ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=jazz-blues-comp-C`

**Pieces**

- Gymnop√©die No. 1 (Satie) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=gymnopedie-1`
- Ballade, Op. 100 No. 15 (Burgm√ºller) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=burgmuller-ballade`
- Canon in D (Pachelbel) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=canon-in-d`
- ii‚ÄìV‚ÄìI √âtude (swing) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=ii-v-i-etude`
- Maple Leaf Rag ‚Äî A strain (Joplin) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=maple-leaf-rag`
- Prelude in E minor, Op. 28 No. 4 (Chopin) ‚Äî `#/songs?song=chopin-prelude-e-minor`
- When the Saints ‚Äî stride version ‚Äî `#/songs?song=when-the-saints-stride`

**Ear**

- Chord qualities ‚Äî level 4 (everything) ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=chords&level=4`
- Scale types ‚Äî modes and blues by ear ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=scale-type&level=4`
- Cadences ‚Äî including the deceptive V ‚Üí vi ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=cadence&level=3`
- Rhythm dictation ‚Äî swing patterns ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=rhythm-dictation&level=4`

**Rhythm**

- Rhythm ‚Äî level 4 (keep swing honest) ‚Äî `#/rhythm?level=4`

**Sight-reading**

- Sight-reading ‚Äî level 5 (hands together) ‚Äî `#/practice?sight=5`

### The ii‚ÄìV‚ÄìI: how jazz moves

Jazz harmony strings dominant motion together: the ii chord leads to V, V resolves to I. The guide tones ‚Äî each chord‚Äôs 3rd and 7th ‚Äî are the two notes that carry the progression; voice-lead them smoothly and the harmony plays itself. Learn the drill in C and F first, then take it around the circle of fifths.

- ii‚ÄìV‚ÄìI drill in C ‚Äî `#/practice?lesson=jazz-251-C`
- Hear it as music in the √©tude ‚Äî `#/songs?song=ii-v-i-etude`

### Modes: old scales, new colours

Play the white keys from D to D and you get Dorian ‚Äî a minor scale with a bright 6th, the sound of Scarborough Fair and half of jazz. From G to G is Mixolydian: a major scale with a lowered 7th, the sound of the blues and rock. Modes are not new notes to learn, just familiar keys heard from a different home base ‚Äî which is why training your ear to spot them matters more than memorizing them.

- D Dorian in the scale library ‚Äî `#/scales?root=D&type=dorian`
- G Mixolydian in the scale library ‚Äî `#/scales?root=G&type=mixolydian`
- Quiz: name the scale type by ear ‚Äî `#/quizzes?mode=scale-type&level=4`

### Where to go from here

Beyond this stage the priorities shift to tone, pedalling, dynamics and interpretation ‚Äî things a microphone-based app cannot fully hear. Keep the daily technique routine, keep sight-reading a level below your pieces, and if you have not already, this is the point where working with a teacher (even occasionally) accelerates everything.

### A week at this stage (~60 min/day)

- 15 min ‚Äî scales & arpeggios: all keys on rotation, two octaves hands together
- 10 min ‚Äî jazz: ii‚ÄìV‚ÄìI in two keys, blues scale improvisation over the comping track
- 25 min ‚Äî repertoire: one long-term piece (Canon/Chopin) plus one lighter piece
- 5 min ‚Äî sight-reading level 5
- 5 min ‚Äî ear training level 4

### Beyond the app

- [Consider a human teacher](https://www.abrsm.org/en-gb/find-a-music-teacher) ‚Äî From this stage on, occasional lessons (even monthly) catch technique problems an app cannot hear ‚Äî posture, tension, tone. Worth it if you can.
- [Josh Wright (YouTube)](https://www.youtube.com/@joshwrightpiano) ‚Äî Concert pianist; deep dives on technique problems once you have the basics.
- [ABRSM Piano syllabus](https://www.abrsm.org/en-gb/our-exams/piano-exams) ‚Äî The graded exam repertoire lists this guide is aligned to ‚Äî browse pieces at your stage even if you never sit an exam.
- [RCM Piano syllabus](https://www.rcmusic.com/en/learning/academic-programs/certificate-program/piano) ‚Äî The North American equivalent of ABRSM; its repertoire lists are a goldmine of level-appropriate pieces.
- [IMSLP (Petrucci Music Library)](https://imslp.org) ‚Äî Free public-domain sheet music ‚Äî every classical piece in this app and thousands more.
- [MuseScore](https://musescore.com) ‚Äî Community sheet music; export MusicXML and import it into the Songs screen to practice with feedback.
- [Open Studio (YouTube)](https://www.youtube.com/@OpenStudioJazz) ‚Äî When the jazz side hooks you: real jazz piano pedagogy from working musicians.

### Move on when‚Ä¶

- You can sit down and play two contrasting pieces from memory, musically
- ii‚ÄìV‚ÄìI voicings come out without thinking in at least four keys
- You are choosing your own repertoire ‚Äî at which point you have outgrown this guide üéâ
